response:
**Results**

We evaluated ten experimental variants of our proposed method on the validation set, reporting mean validation accuracy and standard deviation across five random seeds for each configuration (Table 1). Among these variants, variant_03 serves as the baseline configuration, which excludes the proposed component. The baseline achieved a mean validation accuracy of 67.8% (± 0.6).

Of the nine variants incorporating the proposed component, only variant_08 outperformed the baseline, achieving a mean validation accuracy of 70.4% (± 0.5), representing an absolute improvement of +2.6 percentage points over the baseline. This result demonstrates the potential effectiveness of the proposed modification when combined with the specific settings used in variant_08.

The remaining eight experimental variants with the proposed component (variant_01, variant_02, variant_04, variant_05, variant_06, variant_07, variant_09, and variant_10) failed to surpass the baseline performance. Specifically, their mean validation accuracies were as follows: variant_01 (67.2% ± 0.8), variant_02 (66.9% ± 0.7), variant_04 (65.1% ± 0.9), variant_05 (66.3% ± 1.0), variant_06 (64.7% ± 0.7), variant_07 (65.8% ± 0.8), variant_09 (66.5% ± 0.7), and variant_10 (65.9% ± 0.8). These results indicate that the majority of configurations integrating the proposed component did not yield improvements and, in some cases, resulted in notable performance degradation relative to the baseline.

In summary, our results show that while most variants incorporating the proposed component did not outperform the baseline, a single configuration (variant_08) achieved a substantial and consistent accuracy gain, highlighting the importance of careful configuration selection when applying the proposed approach.
